Research: {"paper_title":"CRISPR-Cas12a Gene Editing of HBG1 and HBG2 Promoters to Treat β-Thalassemia","authors":"Not specified in abstract","source":"PubMed (NCT05444894)","publication_date":"2024","study_type":"Phase 1-2, multicenter, open-label, single-group clinical trial","summary":"This study evaluates renizgamglogene autogedtemcel (reni-cel), an investigational CRISPR-Cas12a gene-edited autologous hematopoietic stem-cell therapy designed to treat transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia by disrupting BCL11A binding sites in HBG1 and HBG2 promoters, thereby reactivating fetal hemoglobin production.","key_findings":{"participants":9 patients (ages 18-35) with transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia,"genotypes":"4 β0/β0 or β0/β0-like; 5 non-β0/β0","follow_up":"Median 17.5 months (range 3.8-23.4 months)","engraftment":"100% neutrophil and platelet engraftment by day 42","transfusion_independence":"All 9 participants became transfusion-free; 6 evaluable at 12+ months remained transfusion-independent","hemoglobin_levels":"Mean total Hb >12 g/dL and fetal Hb >11 g/dL between months 6-18","safety":"69 grade 3/4 adverse events reported; 6 serious adverse events (infections, pyrexia, pneumonitis) in 4 participants; 1 patient with decreased lymphocytes attributed to reni-cel"},"methodology":"Participants received myeloablative conditioning with busulfan before reni-cel infusion. Primary endpoints were neutrophil engraftment by day 42 and adverse event frequency/severity. The study was terminated early based on sponsor's reassessment of clinical development priorities, and reported results were not prespecified.","potential_applications":["Treatment of transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia","Potential application to other hemoglobinopathies (e.g., sickle cell disease)","Autologous gene therapy approach minimizing graft-versus-host disease risk","Possible expansion to younger patient populations"],"conclusions":"Reni-cel treatment demonstrated rapid neutrophil engraftment, significant fetal hemoglobin expression, and complete transfusion independence in all participants. Adverse events were consistent with myeloablative conditioning. These findings support continued investigation of Cas12a gene editing of HBG1/HBG2 promoters for β-thalassemia treatment.","limitations":["Small sample size (n=9)","Non-prespecified analysis due to early study termination","Limited long-term follow-up data","Single-arm study design without control group"],"research_significance":"This represents one of the first clinical applications of CRISPR-Cas12a technology for hemoglobin disorders, demonstrating the potential of gene editing to achieve functional cure of β-thalassemia through fetal hemoglobin reactivation. The high rate of transfusion independence with manageable safety profile is clinically meaningful for patients with limited treatment options."}

Research: ## Research Report: CRISPR-Cas12a Gene Editing of HBG1 and HBG2 Promoters to Treat β-Thalassemia

Research: Reni-cel demonstrates promising proof-of-concept for CRISPR-Cas12a-mediated gene editing of HBG1/HBG2 promoters in treating transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia. All patients achieved transfusion independence with rapid engraftment and significant increases in fetal hemoglobin. While adverse events were consistent with myeloablative conditioning and serious events were manageable, the early trial termination limits conclusions. The data support continued investigation of Cas12a gene editing approaches for β-hemoglobinopathies.

Research: ## Research Report: CRISPR-Cas12a Gene Editing of HBG1 and HBG2 Promoters to Treat β-Thalassemia

Research: This study evaluates renizgamglogene autogedtemcel (reni-cel), an investigational CRISPR-Cas12a gene-edited autologous hematopoietic stem-cell therapy designed to treat transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia by reactivating fetal hemoglobin production through disruption of BCL11A binding sites in HBG1 and HBG2 promoters.

Research: This paper reports on a phase 1-2 clinical trial of renizgamglogene autogedtemcel (reni-cel), an investigational CRISPR-Cas12a gene-edited autologous hematopoietic stem-cell therapy designed to treat transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia by disrupting BCL11A binding sites in the HBG1 and HBG2 promoters to reactivate fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production.
